data.title: 4 Of The Hottest Great American SWANs For The 4th Of July
data.description:
Today, I wanted to provide readers with a special July 4th SWAN Edition.

In case you aren’t familiar with the SWAN term, it stands for “sleeping well at night”.

Over the years (15 on Seeking Alpha), I’ve used the SWAN term frequently to describe some of the best “stress-free” investments to own.

And to qualify as a SWAN, the company must have undeniable blue-chip attributes such as:

  • Proven management
  • Track record of deploying capital to create shareholder value
  • Strong balance sheet
  • Good corporate governance

As in all SWAN recommendations, I like to see consistent earnings because they’re a proxy for cash flow. I’ve found that paying attention to profit margins is one of the best ways to help me avoid pitfalls and to buy shares in high-quality stocks cheaply.

Buying earnings cheaply is one of the best ways to grow your nest egg and is the best stress-free route to investment success.

So, in honor of Independence Day, I decided (with the help of Kody Kester) to put together a list of 4 Great American SWANs.

I hope you enjoy the article and I look forward to your comments below.

Realty Income (O): The Great American Dividend Machine

Whether you love or hate Realty Income, there’s no denying the fact that this REIT delivers on its promise of paying predictable monthly dividends.

While others have tried to replicate the business model by trying to entice investors into the monthly dividend mantra, they’ve failed because they didn’t have the “wide moat” attributes associated with Realty Income’s highly sustainable business model.

For example, Gladstone Commercial (GOOD), a weaker net lease REIT peer, that also pays monthly dividends, was forced to cut its dividend because of eroding earnings driven by a riskier balance sheet and higher cost of capital.

EPR Properties (EPR) is another net lease REIT peer that didn’t have the same diversification attributes as Realty Income and was forced to suspend its dividend during COVID-19 because of its elevated concentration of movie theaters within the portfolio.

As the largest net lease REIT in the world, scale certainly has advantages for Realty Income.

The S&P 500 constituent has over 15,600 properties spread across 50 states, Europe, and the United Kingdom, which provides the company with meaningful operator and geographic diversification.

Many consider scale a disadvantage given the larger denominator of assets under management; however, the benefits of scale can be best illustrated by examining the impressive earnings history that includes 98.2% average occupancy since 2010 and 29 out of 29 years of positive total operational returns.

Those who criticize Realty Income for its size seem to forget the scale advantage, which is a key driver of the successful dividend record in which the company has paid and increased dividends for 30 years in a row.

Of course, scale is not possible with judicious capital management practices.

Realty Income has one of the best balance sheets in the entire REIT sector, rated A3 (Moody’s) and A- (S&P), with solid leverage metrics, including 5.4x net debt to EBITDAre, 4.7x fixed charge coverage, and 35% net debt to total enterprise value.

Although Mr. Market views Realty Income as just another net lease REIT, I view the company through a different lens.

As seen below, weighing 50% for 2025 and 2026 is how I arrive at a fair value of $71 a share (19% discount from the current $58 share price).

This could generate a 29% total return through June 2026 and an 11% annual total return potential over the next five years, making Realty Income an easy SWAN!

Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL): A Dividend Growth All-Star In The Making

Next up on our list is none other than the mega-cap stock, Alphabet. GOOGL boasts a market cap exceeding $2 trillion, and it's not hard to see why. Its platforms are used daily by billions of users.

Why are we confident that the tech juggernaut has plenty of room to run higher in the years to come? Well, GOOGL is positioning itself to establish or maintain itself as a leader in many industries set for substantial growth.

One big picture growth avenue is the global digital advertising market. Greater availability of smartphones around the world and rising social media usage are secular trends that are expected to drive growth in this vertical. That's why The Business Research Company anticipates that the digital advertising industry will compound from $734.2 billion in 2024 revenue to top $1.4 trillion in 2029.

In other words, simply maintaining its share in this market could generate double-digit percentage annual revenue growth for GOOGL in what is currently its core business. From our perspective, the company's platforms, like Google Search and YouTube, are more than capable of doing so.

GOOGL isn't just standing idly by as the leading digital advertising company, either.

As I detailed last month, the company rolled out Gemini 2.5 in Q1 2025. CEO Sundar Pichai noted that active users in AI Studio and Gemini API were up 200% since the start of 2025 alone.

The launch of AI Overviews in 2024 is also helping GOOGL to remain competitive. Despite competition from chatbots, this is what propelled the company's Google Search business to grow search queries and revenue at a healthy rate in Q1 2025.

Growth in cloud computing figures to be another major growth driver for GOOGL. Fortune Business Insights projects that the global cloud computing industry revenue will compound by 16.6% annually from $781.3 billion in 2025 to $2.3 trillion by 2032. That's thanks to increased AI penetration and the ongoing global shift to cloud spending.

Google Cloud put up impressive growth in Q1 2025. Continued capex allocation and the complementary Wiz acquisition should deliver further growth for GOOGL.

By themselves, these are meaningful and immediate growth catalysts for the company. Yet, fellow SA analyst Oliver Rodzianko also thinks Waymo could grow to become a key component of GOOGL over the next 10 to 15 years.

For these reasons, we think the FAST Graphs analyst consensus for 11.4% annual diluted EPS growth off the 2024 base through 2027 is realistic.

GOOGL's balance sheet is also quite vigorous. The company earns an AA+ credit rating from S&P with a stable outlook.

These factors put GOOGL in an excellent position to become a major dividend growth stock over the long haul. This is because the payout ratio is going to be in the high-single-digit percentage range in 2025. That makes up for a 0.5% starting yield, which is less than the S&P 500 index's 1.2% yield.

GOOGL's shares are also a solid value. At the current $180 share price (as of July 3rd, 2025), the stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 18.2 (50% weight for 2025 and 50% for 2026).

This is well under the 10-year average P/E ratio of 24.9 per FAST Graphs and 19% below our fair value P/E ratio of 22.4 ($221 fair value estimate).

If GOOGL meets growth expectations and returns to fair value, it could have a 24% upside by the end of June 2026 and generate 13% annual total returns through 2030 (all info in this subhead was from sources noted above and accompanying hyperlinks).

Globe Life (GL): A Future Dividend Aristocrat

Our third pick for today is the insurance holding company, Globe Life. GL mostly provides lower-middle-income to middle-income consumers with life and supplemental health insurance policies. This is done through direct-to-consumer channels, as well as independent and exclusive agents.

For a better idea of GL's size and scale, it had $17.2 billion of policies in force as of March 31st, 2025. The company's Life segment generated nearly three-quarters of overall TTM profitability. The remaining quarter of TTM segment profitability came from the Health and Investment segments.

Moving forward, GL has a few levers that it can pull to fuel growth. Co-CEO Matt Darden noted in his opening remarks during the Q4 2024 Earnings Call these growth catalysts.

For one, there's a major opportunity to reach the large pool of underinsured or uninsured people in these marketplaces. As GL reaches these people through its various channels, we believe it can pick up more policyholders over time.

Then there's also the fact that the monthly premiums for GL's policies tend to be in the $30 to $60 range. Coupled with the peace of mind that these policies provide to policyholders, this routinely leads to high retention rates. On this note, more than 90% of the company's premium revenue was generated from policies sold in previous years.

It gets better, too. These affordable monthly premiums give GL some room for premium hikes. That should be another tailwind for the company to produce growth.

Finally, GL is committed to enhancing shareholder returns through its buyback program. Each year, the company allocates hundreds of millions of dollars to share buybacks, retiring a few percent of its share count.

These elements explain why the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for diluted net income per share to rise by 10.2% annually through 2027 off the 2024 base of $12.37.

GL's 0.9% dividend yield may be modest, but don't sell it short. Like GOOGL, the company's payout ratio is set to be in the high single-digit percentage range for 2025. This is why we think GL appears poised to build on its 20-year dividend growth streak for the foreseeable future, potentially becoming a Dividend Aristocrat in 2030.

GL also benefits from an A credit rating from S&P with a stable outlook.

The insurer also looks to be a bargain. From the current $121 share price, the stock is priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of just 8.5 (50% weight for 2025 and 50% weight for 2026).

That's materially less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 13.9 per FAST Graphs and a 24% discount to our fair value P/E ratio of 11.1 ($159 fair value).

If GL reverts to fair value and live up to the growth consensus, it could have a 33% upside ahead through June 2026 and a 14% annual total return potential by the end of 2030 (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all details in this subhead were from Globe Life's Q1 2025 Investor Update Presentation).

Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO): A Business Powering Scientific Progress

Last but not least, we have Thermo Fisher Scientific. This company is a leading player in the life sciences industry, generating $42.9 billion in total revenue over the last four quarters.

That revenue was also geographically diversified, with about half coming from North America and the other half from Europe, Asia-Pacific, and the rest of the world.

TMO provides a variety of products and services to pharma and biotech customers, academic and government customers, and diagnostics and healthcare customers. These include reagents, instruments, software, consumables, diagnostic test kits, and lab equipment.

In other words, TMO makes the groundbreaking research and development being carried out by its customers possible. As the global economy grows and the population ages, demand for medicines and vaccines will likely continue to rise.

An investment in TMO is a bet that this higher demand for healthcare will be reflected in higher R&D spending from pharmaceutical companies. This should help the company's revenue and adjusted EPS to climb higher. As a result, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is that adjusted EPS will grow by 9.7% annually off the 2024 base of $21.86 through 2027.

TMO's 0.4% dividend yield doesn't stand out. However, the payout ratio is positioned to be around 8% in 2025. That should give TMO the flexibility to extend its 8-year dividend growth streak.

The company also has an A- credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook.

TMO's shares are an enticing value, too. At the current $429 share price, the stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 18.3 (50% weight for 2025 and 50% for 2026). This is beneath the 10-year average P/E ratio of 22.2 per FAST Graphs and 18% under our fair value multiple ($521 fair value estimate).

If TMO matches growth expectations and returns to fair value, it could post a 22% total return over the next 12 months and have a 13% annual total return potential through 2030 (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to Thermo Fisher Scientific's Annual Shareholder Meeting Presentation and Thermo Fisher Scientific's 10-K Filing).

Risks To Consider

O, GOOGL, GL, and TMO are businesses with robust fundamentals. Even so, they all have risks that could alter the investment thesis.

O's key risks include potential defaults from major tenants and unfavorable economic or demographic trends in key markets.

The REIT balances the first risk through tenant diversification. However, if any key tenants encounter financial difficulties, it could take time to find a viable substitute. In the meantime, that could weigh on O's financial results.

As I (Kody) outlined in a recent article, the REIT derived 36.9% of its total annualized base rent from its top five markets (page 51 of 59 of O's Q1 2025 10-Q Filing). Any trends, like a lack of economic growth or population loss, may impact fundamentals via reduced occupancy and market rents in such markets.

Pertinent risks for GOOGL include a potential cyberbreach, revenue cyclicality, and significant competition.

A widespread cyber breach could jeopardize the sensitive data of the company's users and advertisers. Such an occurrence could lead users/advertisers to flock to competitors.

It could also cause massive lawsuits/fines to be brought against GOOGL. Much of the company's revenue is derived from digital advertising. While generally not as vulnerable to recession as traditional advertising, GOOGL would likely still be impacted via a temporary deceleration in revenue and diluted EPS growth.

Lastly, the company seems to be executing well to emerge as a winner in the ongoing AI arms race. If that doesn't prove to be the case, however, GOOGL's growth profile could be damaged.

GL's major risks include underwriting and claims risks, liquidity risk, and potential data breaches.

The company must walk the tightrope as an insurer. GL has to balance pricing its products high enough for the assumed risk, while also remaining competitive. If it prices excessively, the company risks losing out to competition.

If it prices inadequately, a surge in claims from natural disasters or pandemics could pressure profitability. In a worst-case scenario, claims to be paid out could exceed its insurance float.

Finally, like GOOGL, GL also has a substantial amount of sensitive data on its IT networks. If this is compromised via a cybersecurity breach, that could be very costly to the company in terms of lawsuits and damage to its brand image.

Notable risks for TMO include a slowdown in biopharma spending and industry competition. Any potential pharmaceutical reform could result in reduced industry spending, which could trickle down to the company as a consequence.

TMO also must continue to innovate and work to provide the products and services that clients demand. If it fails to do so, it could lose market share to competitors like Danaher (DHR) and Agilent Technologies (A).

Summary: Four Great American Companies With Potentially Explosive Upside

In our view, the four companies highlighted today are of world-class quality. O, GOOGL, and TMO each earn 13/13 Ultra SWAN quality ratings in the Zen Research Terminal. GL is no slouch, either, with a 12/13 Super SWAN quality rating.

The four provide a blended dividend yield of approximately 1.8%, which sits moderately above the S&P 500 index's 1.2% yield.

The blended average payout ratio in the mid 20% range is also well below the mid 60% range that rating agencies think is sustainable for their industries. That's why we believe that each of these companies is well-positioned for more dividend growth in the years ahead.

The four businesses are financially sound, too.

Each company has an A or AA-rated balance sheet from S&P. Overall, the average risk of bankruptcy over the next 30 years for the group is estimated at just 1.5%, per The Zen Research Terminal.

Best of all, we think the stocks trade at valuations that offer sizable 12-month upside potential. To this point, shares are priced 18% to 24% discounts to our fair value estimates.

If they revert to fair value and match growth expectations, each could generate a 22% to 33% total return through June 2026. Over the next five years, the annual total return potential remains strong as well, varying from 11% for O to 14% for GL.

Thank you for reading, and God Bless America.

Happy SWAN Investing!

data.title: Brady Corporation: Steady Growth, Strong Margins, And Strategic Resilience
data.description:
Executive Summary

Brady Corporation (NYSE:BRC) is a leading manufacturer and supplier of identification solutions and workplace safety products with a broad international footprint. The company has demonstrated steady revenue and earnings growth, strong profitability, and a disciplined capital allocation. Recently, Brady posted record adjusted EPS benefitting from organic sales growth in the Americas and Asia and accretive acquisitions while managing macro headwinds in Europe and currency pressures. With a trailing twelve-month (TTM) revenue of $1.46 billion and net income of $194 million (net margin 13.35%), Brady’s balance sheet remains strong—net debt is modest, cash generation is robust, and the company has continued a 39-year streak of consecutive annual dividend increases. Although facing uncertainties around global tariffs and soft industrial demand in some regions Brady’s diversified business, operational efficiencies, and disciplined M&A provide a resilient foundation. Shares trade at 17.1× trailing earnings, 2.3× sales, and 2.8× book with a 1.4% dividend yield and a payout ratio of just 24%. Compared to similar companies Brady trades at a premium on profitability but remains more conservatively managed and less leveraged, supporting a positive long-term outlook.

Company Overview and Background

Brady Corporation, founded in 1914 and headquartered in Milwaukee is a global leader in identification solutions (IDS) and workplace safety (WPS). Its broad portfolio includes safety signs, labels, floor-marking tape, RFID/barcode scanners, lockout/tagout devices, software for safety compliance, printing systems, and specialty labels used across manufacturing, healthcare, chemicals, automotive, energy, and government. Products are distributed via direct sales, distributors, and digital channels, serving customers in over 100 countries. As of July 2024, Brady employed approximately 5,700 people worldwide. The company’s approach blends organic innovation with strategic acquisitions, recently acquiring Gravotech and Funai’s Microfluidic Solutions business to enhance its direct part marking and inkjet technology capabilities. Brady's decentralized manufacturing model, with most production close to the point of sale, enables flexibility and mitigates tariff impacts.

Strategic Position and Future Outlook

Brady's growth strategy is anchored by three pillars. Organic sales growth through R&D, targeted M&A in adjacent technologies, and operational efficiency. Over the past year, organic sales in the Americas and Asia grew 5.4% offsetting a weakening in Europe and Australia (where sales declined 5.4% due to macroeconomic conditions). Recent acquisitions of Gravotech and Funai have broadened Brady's offering in industrial part marking and inkjet, building out its portfolio to serve both legacy and emerging markets.

The company's R&D investment has ramped up with $19.2 million spent in Q3 2025 (up 8.5% YoY) fueling new product launches such as the i6100 industrial desktop label printer and the HH86 RFID reader—designed for rugged high-volume environments. Management has highlighted strong growth in specialty identification and optical reader technologies, with particular strength in Asia (nearly 23% organic sales growth in Q3 2025). While Europe remains challenged Brady has taken proactive cost actions and expects stabilization and eventual improvement as industrial conditions recover.

Looking forward, management is guiding for full-year adjusted diluted EPS of $4.48–$4.63 low-single digit organic sales growth and ongoing investment in product development and operational efficiency. The diversified revenue base (52% US, 30% Europe, 8% Asia, 10% Australia/other Americas) and the company’s flexible manufacturing footprint help manage risks around tariffs and global supply chains.

Financial Health

Brady maintains a strong financial position underpinned by healthy cash flows, low leverage, and disciplined capital management: Their revenues were $1.46 billion up 11.4% YoY in the most recent quarter, with acquisitions contributing 10.5% and organic growth of 1.6% (Income Statement). Operating Margin is 18.6% with adjusted operating income up 20.2% in the Americas/Asia segment in the last quarter. Total debt/total capitalization is just 13.8%; net debt is effectively minimal with $152 million in cash and $162 million in debt. In Q3 2025, Brady repurchased $33.2 million in shares and paid $44.5 million to shareholders via dividends and buybacks. The dividend yield is 1.41% with a low payout ratio (23.6%), and the company recently marked its 39th consecutive annual increase (Statistics). Management’s conservative approach ensures financial flexibility to pursue strategic growth and weather macro shocks.

Metric BRC CMPR DLX EBF QUAD GEO
Revenue (TTM, $M) 1,460 3,370 2,120 389 2,650 2,420
Revenue Growth (YoY) 11.4% 1.1% 0.3% -5.7% -3.9% -0.2%
Operating Margin (TTM) 18.6% 5.2% 10.9% 13.7% 4.1% 10.1%
Net Margin (TTM) 13.4% 4.6% 2.6% 10.1% -0.6% 1.2%
ROE (TTM) 17.8% -- 9.1% 12.0% -19.3% 2.2%
ROA (TTM) 10.2% 7.8% 5.2% 8.2% 5.9% 5.0%
Debt/Equity (%) 13.8% -- 249% 1.4% 633% 132%
Price/Earnings (TTM) 17.1× 8.4× 13.1× 12.3× -- 111×
EV/EBITDA (TTM) 11.4× 7.3× 6.2× 6.7× 5.8× 15.4×
Price/Sales (TTM) 2.3× 0.4× 0.3× 1.2× 0.1× 1.4×
Price/Book (TTM) 2.8× -- 1.2× 1.6× 3.3× 2.6×
Dividend Yield (Forward) 1.41% 0% 7.5% 5.5% 5.3% 0%
Current Ratio 1.87× 0.65× 0.93× 2.97× 0.96× 1.29×
FCF Margin (TTM) 12.1% 5.7% 9.5% 10.0% 2.8% 3.3%

Brady’s operating and net margins (18.6% and 13.4%) are industry-leading, well above those of their peers except Ennis which also maintains a high net margin (10%). Brady's ROE (17.8%) and ROA (10.2%) are again above their industry peers reflecting its efficient operations and prudent capital management. Brady posted robust revenue growth (11.4% YoY), outpacing all comparable firms. The company trades at a premium (P/E 17.1×, EV/EBITDA 11.4×), reflecting its higher profitability and lower risk. Peers like Cimpress and Deluxe are cheaper on a P/E and EV/EBITDA basis but in my opinion carry much higher leverage and weaker returns. It appears that Brady’s leverage is conservative with debt/equity sitting at 13.8% in comparison to its peers which are considerably higher. Its dividend yield is lower than its peers but the low payout and buyback activity may suggest capacity for future increases. Its 39-year record of dividend growth is unmatched among peers.

Key Risks and Challenges

Brady is exposed to incremental tariffs on certain imported products (~$3M expense in Q3 2025), though mitigated by geographic manufacturing diversity. Further escalation in trade tensions could affect input costs or demand, but management has demonstrated agility in shifting sourcing and pricing. Europe and Australia remain soft industrial markets impacting organic growth. Prolonged weakness could dampen results though cost actions have helped offset revenue declines. While recent acquisitions are accretive and synergistic, integration risks remain, especially as the company expands its technological portfolio. Like any other global company, Brady faces FX risk and inflationary pressures.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Brady Corporation stands out as a high-quality industrial company combining strong organic growth, high profitability, strong and disciplined capital allocation and a stable balance sheet. The company's operational discipline and diversified global footprint enable it to navigate cyclical headwinds and capitalize on emerging opportunities such as specialty identification, RFID, and industrial printing. Brady's financial results are strong, and many metrics are above peer averages. They have strong margins, high returns and prudent leverage. Although shares trade at a premium to some more leveraged peers, there is some justification for Brady’s stability, dividend track record, and growth prospects. I believe that the risk profile is low, and the company has multiple levers such as organic growth, M&A, efficiency, and capital return to drive shareholder value. For long-term investors who seek a mix of defensiveness and steady compounding in the industrial technology industry, I believe that Brady merits a positive outlook.

data.title: Up To The Task Of A Twisting And Turning Market
data.description:
By Eli Pars, CFA

Review

There’s certainly been no shortage of volatility in the markets this year—to both the upside and the downside. And the second quarter seemed to last a lot longer than three months! The quarter kicked off with President Trump’s “Liberation Day” announcement on April 2, catching investors off guard and sending markets into a tailspin. However, as investors digested details of the US government’s tariff proposals and the Trump administration refined what it was seeking, markets climbed back up. Even the outbreak of war between Iran and Israel couldn’t slow the global equity market down for long, and the MSCI World Index gained 9.75% for the year-to-date.

The global convertible market has more than kept pace with the global equity market so far this year, with the FTSE Global Convertible Index rising 11.17% through June. This year-to-date strength builds on the year’s first quarter, when convertible securities also outperformed equities, and global markets outperformed the US across both asset classes.

Convertibles don’t usually capture 115% of an equity move of this magnitude (up ~10%), as they have so far this year. This capture seems driven in part by a reversal of the Magnificent 7-type outperformance of mega-cap equities in recent years. (Broad equity benchmarks include greater representation to mega-caps than the convertible market; our whitepaper, “The Case for Strategic Convertible Allocations,” explores the market-cap differences between equities and convertibles—and the impact on performance—at greater length.)

After outperforming the FTSE Global Convertible Index in the first quarter, the fund trailed in the second quarter, even though it posted a robust gain in absolute terms. An underweight to Europe and an overweight to Chinese consumer names—both of which have helped in the past—hindered relative performance during the second quarter. As active managers, we continually assess our holdings and believe our positioning still makes sense as we look forward.

Issuance

Global convertible issuance surged in June with $26.1 billion in new convertibles coming to market, bringing quarterly issuance to $47.1 billion and year-to-date issuance to nearly $70.5 billion. We continue to forecast a strong new issue calendar for the remainder of the year.

As we have noted in past commentary, the pace of issuance could be more measured if equity markets stay volatile, but we believe the interest-rate environment continues to incentivize issuers to choose convertibles over nonconvertible debt to manage borrowing costs. Over recent quarters, the participation of investment-grade companies and greater regional diversification have energized the convertible market, and we would welcome a continuation of these trends.

Year-to-Date Global Convertible Issuance ($ bil)

Positioning

Calamos Global Convertible Fund (CXGCX) favors companies that offer a healthy measure of downside risk mitigation and upside equity exposure. We have maintained a significant overweight to consumer discretionary companies, as well as an aforementioned overweight to Chinese issues. These overweights are driven by convertible structures and equity valuations—in other words, by bottom-up security selection, not macro calls. On average, the fund’s portfolio has a slightly higher delta, but what we believe is a better risk-reward profile.

Conclusion

While the S&P 500 flirts with new highs, multiple potential sources of volatility could materialize in the coming months. Whether it is tariff deadlines, war in the Middle East, or the sale or ban of TikTok, plenty of things may stir the pot.

We’re ready for more twists and turns, but this is an environment that we believe plays to our experience and strengths. We’ve invested through many periods of turmoil and believe there is always opportunity.

Original Post

data.title: Subsea 7: Margins Still Rising
data.description:
Subsea 7 S.A. (OTCPK:ACGYF)(OTCPK:SUBCY) has been benefiting from continued positive mix effects related to the age and risk of projects, as well as picking up new significant orders that will be beefing up the backlog in the coming quarter. Subsea 7 has relatively outperformed Saipem SpA (OTCPK:SAPMF), reflecting our belief that in the merger between them, the Subsea 7 shareholders are getting the better deal. In all, strategic considerations for oil remain an important driver and the backlog is likely to remain robust, with margin lift likely on the current backlog. We think there are real benefits to the merger, and we don't mind the outlook too much, but we are aware of the risks to the general oil market and the sustainable generation of particularly greenfield backlog. The price has gone up a lot since we started covering the company, and it is cyclical.

Latest Results

As a reminder, Subsea 7 is an EPC company that does development projects offshore to develop oil and gas assets, particularly subsea infrastructure and pipe laying projects for offshore oil and gas.

Revenues are clearly up as project deliveries proceed. EBITDA is growing ahead of revenue nicely on a couple of factors.

  • The later backlog, quoted after 2022, includes indexing clauses that have increased the profitability of these quoted EPC projects. Recall that Subsea 7 takes the risks associated with delivering a project profitably, but indexing in the contracts has been introduced since the 2022 inflation period started which has reduced risk and meant that more recent quoted projects are liquidating more profitably than earlier projects. The mix is skewing towards newer projects.
  • The mix is also skewing towards brownfield projects, which are more incremental, less risky, and therefore more profitable than greenfield projects.
  • Moreover, the mix is skewed towards projects that are further along in their delivery, where the later stages of projects usually liquidate more profitably as the foundational and more intensive work is already done.

They are still winning considerable new business since the close of Q1. Just in May, the company announced three major projects that are valued ahead of the $1.5 billion mark for the backlog. We expect this to mean solid book-to-bill ratios, likely around the 1x mark.

Bottom Line

As mentioned, Saipem had a lower multiple at the moment when the merger between them and Subsea 7 was announced. However, the merger stipulated that the merged company would be owned 50:50 by each company's shareholders. This meant that Subsea 7 shareholders were getting more ownership of their EBITDA. We felt this was unfortunate for Saipem which is experiencing an inflection in profitability. Saipem climbed around 12% versus more than 25% for Subsea 7 shareholders since the merger announcement, both reflecting the better fleet economics that will come from a combined entity, but the additional growth in Subsea 7 likely reflects the quality of the deal for them. We felt that the value given to Subsea 7 shareholders was about worth a control premium, so the more than 10% relative outperformance reflects quite a lot of the value that was embedded in those shares at that point, meaning the relative value between the stocks has closed a bit since we discussed the matter.

The question then is on earnings direction. Saipem and Subsea 7 are quite similar businesses, so the matter of the positive mix effects means that the earnings case for these businesses on an underlying basis is quite good. However, we wonder about the sustainable development of greenfield demand given that oil prices have come down. $60 per barrel is often the breakeven of offshore developments in most places. The really big projects that came in lately were brownfield, meaning that there is some reticence about sustained high oil prices particularly as OPEC+ is coming back with their supply. On the discussed mix benefits, margins are expected to improve, but sales aren't suspected to be rising that much.

The margin improvements are important and will be sustained for as long as the current backlog is in play. But we know that this industry is cyclical and therefore aren't eager to jump on at this exact moment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: French Economy Under Significant Strain With No Signs Of Recovery
data.description:
By Charlotte de Montpellier

France lags behind its European peers

While some eurozone economies performed surprisingly well in the first half of 2024, France continues to disappoint. The latest industrial production figures confirm the ongoing deterioration in economic momentum.

In May 2025, manufacturing output declined by 1.0% month-on-month, following a revised 0.7% drop in April. Total industrial production fell by 0.5%, after a 1.4% contraction the previous month. These figures came in well below expectations. Over the past three months, manufacturing output is now 0.4% lower than during the same period in 2024.

Most industrial sectors saw declines in May, with the exception of transport equipment (aerospace, shipbuilding, rail) and electricity production. Construction output also fell by 0.5% for the second consecutive month. Over the last three months, construction activity is down 3.8% year-on-year.

These figures paint a worrying picture of the French industrial sector and suggest that the economy is under significant strain. We now expect GDP to contract by 0.1% quarter-on-quarter in the second quarter.

No signs of a turnaround

What’s more concerning is the lack of any meaningful recovery signals. Business sentiment in the industrial sector weakened further in June and remains subdued across the broader economy. With confidence low and demand expectations muted, a rebound in industrial output looks unlikely in the near term.

Several headwinds are weighing on the outlook:

  • Trade uncertainty: With the 9 July deadline looming, there is still no clarity on potential US tariffs on European goods. Exporters remain in a wait-and-see mode, delaying investment and expansion plans.
  • Euro appreciation: Since the start of the year, the euro has appreciated by 5% in nominal effective terms (against 41 key trading partners), 4.3% in real terms, and 13.5% against the US dollar. This is eroding export competitiveness and weighing on production.
  • Geopolitical tensions: Global uncertainty continues to dampen business confidence.
  • Domestic fiscal uncertainty: France is entering a difficult budget negotiation phase. A significant fiscal adjustment appears inevitable, likely involving tax hikes that could affect both households and businesses. The risk of political gridlock – or even a government collapse – could push French bond spreads higher, raising financing costs for firms.

Growth to remain subdued

All in all, the French economy is facing a challenging environment with little sign of improvement ahead. We forecast GDP growth of just 0.4% in 2025, following 1.1% in 2024, with only a modest recovery to 0.8% in 2026.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: MicroStrategy Preferred Stocks Explained
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

In this short article, we'll take a look at MicroStrategy Incorporated's (NASDAQ:MSTR) listed preferred stocks - the 10.00% Series A Perpetual Strife Preferred Stock, MicroStrategy Incorporated 10.00% SER A PERPETUAL STRIFE PFD STK (NASDAQ:STRF), the 8.00% Series A Perpetual Strike Preferred Stock, MicroStrategy Incorporated 8.00% SERIES A PERPETUAL STRIKE PFD (NASDAQ:STRK), and the 10.00% Series A Perpetual Stride Preferred Stock, Perpetual Stride Preferred Stock (NASDAQ:STRD) and the pair trade opportunity they present at the moment to the sophisticated traders/investors.

The pair trade idea

The recent price appreciation of MicroStrategy's listed preferreds prompted us to calculate some option values in an attempt to capitalize on potential mispricings among them as safely as possible. What we have on hand is the 8% nominal yield STRK convertible into 1/10 common shares, and currently priced at $118 with a current yield of 6.73%.

The holders of STRK have an embedded perpetual 1/10th of a call option on the common stock of the company, MSTR. The option is worth exercising, or is "in the money", only if the price of MSTR is more than 10 times the market price of STRK, so we'll start from here. Given the current pricing of the convertible preferred, we have a strike price of $1190 for its embedded perpetual preferred.

For our purposes, it's a difficult task to evaluate the perpetual option value, but we can take a long enough term, let's say a 20-year option, as an approximation. We'll also consider different scenarios for the implied volatility, starting from 40% and going up.

What we can do is see how STRK's yield metric will change if we strip it of its embedded option. STRK is long 1/10 of this call option, so we need to sell it and adjust its distributions over the next 20 years with this additional income.

The 20-year option's call value is around $220, and we need to take 1/10 of it and add 1/20th of this result to STRK's distribution over the 20-year holding period. This means that the annual distribution of STRK, now stripped of its embedded option, has to be increased by $1.10, or we'll have the security with an "option-stripped" nominal yield of 9.1%, or 7.65% CY at its current pricing and 40% implied volatility in the common stock MSTR. This way, we have simulated a traditional preferred stock without an embedded option, and we can compare it to STRF and STRD. Using the options calculator above, we can calculate STRK's CY at different implied volatility scenarios:

Our thesis is that STRK is overvalued at this price compared to its listed brothers. We need a long leg among the two traditional preferreds - STRF and STRD, and an option play, so we have a decent pair trade. First, we'll take a look at STRF:

STRF is offering the investors a 10% nominal yield and is senior to STRK:

the perpetual strife preferred stock will be senior to our class A common stock, class B common stock, and 8.00% Series A perpetual strike preferred stock (“perpetual strike preferred stock”).

Source: STRF Prospectus

Currently, it is priced at $111.02 with a CY of 9.01%, or more than 230 basis points above STRK. It is, however, a traditional preferred stock, and unlike STRF, will not have the price appreciation (and depreciation) of MSTR directly reflected in its price.

The other security we have as a potential long leg in the pair trade is STRD, which we recently covered in detail in an IPO article.

STRD is junior in the capital structure of the company, compared to STRK:

although the STRD Stock will be senior to our class A common stock and class B common stock, it will be junior to our 8.00% Series A perpetual strike preferred stock (“STRK Stock”), our 10.00% Series A perpetual strife preferred stock (“STRF Stock”), and our existing and future indebtedness, structurally junior to the liabilities of our subsidiaries and subject to the rights and preferences of any other class or series of preferred stock then outstanding;

Source: STRD Prospectus

It's currently priced at $89.85 with a current yield of 11.11%, and unlike STRK and STRF, it pays non-cumulative dividends.

In the calculations that follow, we'll give STRK the benefit of the doubt when comparing it to STRF, despite its lower position in the capital structure, and will consider it "option-stripped" fairly valued at the same CY as STRF. STRD is lower in the capital structure and has a non-cumulative distribution, so we'll want a 2% CY spread with STRK. Below are the calculations about the fair value of the "option-stripped" STRK at different implied volatilities, and compared once to STRD and a second time to STRF.

How can one play this opportunity?

It all depends on your expectation of the Implied Volatility of the MSTR and how bullish/bearish/neutral you are on the common stock in the foreseeable future. I'll point out a few potential scenarios that make sense to me and leave it there, as there are just too many factors to be considered, and these factors will most probably be weighted differently by the different investors.

First, if you are somewhat bearish on MSTR or Bitcoin in general, you can just take a long position in STRF/STRD and short in STRK. That way, you are in a position to exploit both opportunities - the pair trade potential in the mispriced preferred stocks and a short position in the perpetual call option on MSTR.

Second, for investors who are somewhat neutral or unsure about the future valuation of MSTR, and want to simply lock the pair trade opportunity that the mispriced preferreds are offering. In this case, I'd take again the pair trade as described above, but will also need a long call option in MSTR to offset the shorted embedded option in STRK. A perpetual option like the one in STRK is impossible to buy on the open market. However, a two-year call option with a strike price of around $1200 is something that can be bought. One way or another, the pair trade should be closed long before the two-year mark expires.

Third, if you are extremely bullish on MSTR, you can simply buy STRD or STRF and 1/10th of a two-year call option in MSTR as described above. This way you simulate holding STRK, but the underlying preferred is yielding more.

Conclusion

We have all the reasons to believe STRK is overvalued at its current price. In this short article, we presented a model valuation for its embedded option, and also some ideas on how traders/investors can try to exploit MSTR's preferreds' current valuations to their benefit. Depending on individuals' expectations about the future price and implied volatility on the common stock of MicroStrategy - MSTR, different approaches might be more or less appropriate.

data.title: NAV Monitor: U.S. Equity REITs Trade At Higher Discount To NAV In June
data.description:
Publicly listed US equity real estate investment trusts ended June at a median 19.1% discount to their consensus net asset value (NAV) per share estimates, 28 basis points up from a median discount of 18.8% as of May 30, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

The hotel sector traded at the largest median discount to NAV at 35.5%, down from a 35.7% median discount to NAV as of May 30. The timber sector traded at a median discount of 33.5%, roughly 2.8 percentage points up from 30.8% in the previous month.

Healthcare REITs continued to trade at the highest median premium, 19.3%, which is about 1.4 percentage points down from the 20.6% median premium to NAV as of May 30.

The communications sector, with three REITs in the analysis, posted the largest monthly increase in valuation. As of June 30, the sector traded at a 0.4% median premium to NAV, up from a 17.2% discount to NAV as of May 30.

Largest discounts

Industrial REIT Industrial Logistics Properties Trust (ILPT) traded at the largest discount to NAV, closing June 30 at $4.55 per share, 58.5% below the consensus NAV estimate of $10.97 per share.

Among the 10 public REITs with at least $200 million in market capitalization trading at the steepest discounts, four belonged to the hotel sector and three were from the office sector.

Hotel REIT RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ), which ranked second, traded at $7.28 per share on June 30, 54.8% below the consensus NAV estimate. Other hotel REITs on the list included Park Hotels & Resorts Inc. (PK), Pebblebrook Hotel Trust (PEB) and Summit Hotel Properties Inc. (INN), which traded at discounts of 51.9%, 48.5% and 47.7%, respectively.

Office REITs on the top discount list included City Office REIT Inc. (CIO), Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN) and Hudson Pacific Properties Inc. (HPP), which closed June 30 at discounts of 54.5%, 49.0% and 41.0%, respectively.

Largest premiums

Healthcare REITs dominated the list of 10 public REITs with at least $200 million in market capitalization that traded at the largest premiums at the end of June.

Healthcare REIT Welltower Inc. (WELL) traded at the largest premium, closing June 30 at $153.73 per share, 92.4% above the consensus NAV estimate of $79.89 per share.

Telecom tower REIT Crown Castle Inc. (CCI) landed the No. 2 spot, trading at $102.73 per share, 72.7% above the consensus NAV estimate of $59.50 per share.

The other healthcare REITs on the list were American Healthcare REIT Inc. (AHR), CareTrust REIT Inc. (CTRE) and Sabra Health Care REIT Inc. (SBRA), ranking third, fourth and fifth, respectively. On June 30, American Healthcare traded at a 52.1% premium, CareTrust traded at a 49.6% premium and Sabra Health traded at a 30.5% premium to NAV.

National Health Investors Inc. (NHI), Omega Healthcare Investors Inc. (OHI) and Medical Properties Trust Inc. (MPW) were the three other healthcare REITs in the eighth through 10th spots.

About this analysis

This Data Dispatch is updated monthly and was last published June 4. The analysis includes US equity real estate investment trusts that trade on the Nasdaq, NYSE or NYSE American with market capitalizations of at least $200 million and can offer insight into how the Street is valuing different property sectors. While valuations within the portfolio of publicly traded REITs might not match all privately owned properties, the public markets can often be a leading indicator for potential future property pricing. That insight is particularly helpful when there is little price discovery in the market due to a lack of transactions.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Summit Therapeutics: AstraZeneca Partnership Could Be A Game-Changer, But Clinical Risks Remain
data.description:
Overview

Summit Therapeutics' stock (NASDAQ:SMMT) is trending following a Bloomberg report that it's in talks with AstraZeneca (AZN) surrounding its investigational drug, ivonescimab. According to the report, a licensing deal could generate "as much as $15 billion over time to license," including a "several billion dollar upfront payment." Highlighting the potential return on investment, recall that Summit’s initial licensing agreement with Akeso for ivonescimab in December 2022 was for up to $5 billion, with $500 million upfront.

Having covered the company a couple of times in the past, the news comes as no surprise. PD-L1/VEGF has been a popular target for big pharma following promising clinical trial data in China, in which ivonescimab outperformed Keytruda in first-line PD-L1+ advanced NSCLC (HARMONi-2), a multibillion-dollar market. Notably, ivonescimab is poised to be first-in-class, nearing pivotal Phase 3 data in the U.S. and having already been approved in China for the second-line treatment of EGFR-mutated, locally advanced or metastatic non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer and recently for first-line PD-L1-positive NSCLC as monotherapy (marketed by Akeso).

In my last article, I noted that ivonescimab has the potential to achieve over $10 billion in peak annual revenue (assuming 50% market share in NSCLC) should data in China translate in the U.S. However, I also noted fierce competition in PD-L1/VEGF with companies like BioNTech (BNTX) and Merck (MRK) joining the race. A month following my article, Pfizer spent $1.25 billion upfront and potentially up to $4.8 billion in milestone payments to license 3SBio’s PD-1/VEGF bispecific antibody, SSGJ-707.

Summit's Cash Runway: Why This Deal Matters Now

For Summit, the deal would provide a major and immediate windfall of cash. As of March 31, 2025, the company had $361.3 million in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments. Notably, advancing ivonescimab through multiple late-stage clinical trials is proving costly. Summit's "net cash used in operating activities" totaled $61.1 million in its first quarter. This implies a cash runway of no more than six quarters. Should such a deal materialize, Summit wouldn't have to worry about cash for quite a while. Interestingly, Summit is reportedly in discussion with “other major pharmaceutical firms,” suggesting widespread interest.

Summit Doubles Down: Revolution Medicines Partnership

Interestingly, the Bloomberg report follows news of Summit reaching a clinical collaboration with Revolution Medicines (RVMD). The two will test their respective drugs in RAS mutant non-small cell lung cancer, pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, and colorectal cancer. So, Summit is essentially "doubling down" on ivonescimab, committing more R&D investments. This also provides some validation for Revolution Medicine's RAS(ON) inhibitor approach, but that's another article.

Ivonescimab's OS Data Gap: The Regulatory Challenge

Taking a closer look at ivonescimab’s progress in clinical trials, it has hit a speed bump when it comes to Overall Survival (OS) in both the HARMONi (international) and the HARMONi-A (conducted in China) trials. Recall that ivonescimab has shown striking and statistically significant progression-free survival (PFS) advantages against Keytruda in NSCLC. However, PFS, while supportive of accelerated approval, doesn’t typically confer full approval and market adoption. On the other hand, OS does.

In HARMONi, despite meeting the primary endpoint of PFS (HR 0.52; p<0.00001) versus chemotherapy plus placebo, OS showed only a positive trend (HR 0.79; p=0.057) at the primary analysis. It is possible that the OS data will mature and become statistically significant soon. Critically, the benefits seen in HARMONi-2 (China) were replicated in HARMONi (an international, multicenter study), relieving some concerns that the data wouldn’t translate overseas.

In HARMONi-A, while the risk of disease progression or death compared to chemotherapy alone saw a 48% reduction, a 21% improvement in OS did not reach the threshold for statistical significance (HR 0.80). So, the mixed data could lengthen U.S. approval times and complicate the regulatory path ahead.

In the meantime, ivonescimab is undergoing two multi-regional Phase 3 trials: HARMONi-3 (ivonescimab + chemo versus Keytruda + chemo in first-line metastatic, squamous and non-squamous NSCLC) and HARMONi-7 (ivonescimab versus Keytruda in first-line metastatic NSCLC patients whose tumors have high PD-L1 expression).

Beyond NSCLC: The $90B Addressable Market

Outside of NSCLC, Summit likes to point out that “the addressable market approaches $90 billion globally (...) across all checkpoint inhibitor indications.” In China, ivonescimab is being evaluated in first-line triple negative breast cancer (Phase 3), first-line biliary tract cancer (Phase 3), first-line pancreatic cancer (Phase 3), and metastatic colorectal cancer (Phase 2), among other solid tumors.

Conclusion

To wrap up, the potential deal with AstraZeneca would be a watershed moment for Summit. Despite mixed clinical trial data so far, ivonescimab has the potential to displace Keytruda in NSCLC. It doesn’t stop there. Summit will await China data in other solid tumors to ascertain whether or not to initiate U.S.-based clinical trials (assuming it still has rights to the drug). This gives it a unique advantage in that Summit can automatically gravitate towards indications with high likelihoods of success, avoiding the "shot-in-the-dark" approach often featured in drug development.

On the other hand, significant uncertainty remains. A deal may not materialize. Mixed OS data might necessitate longer follow-up periods, additional confirmatory trials, or result in a restrictive label. Even if approved, without convincing OS success, ivonescimab may struggle to succeed on the market. Moreover, despite the Bloomberg report, Summit’s stock rallied only 8.5%, suggesting that its $17 billion valuation has already priced in much of ivonescimab’s current value. Lastly, Summit is entirely dependent on ivonescimab’s development. Should it fail to confer OS benefit or should an unexpected safety event manifest, Summit’s stock could crater.

For me, recent events support my bullish thesis on SMMT (maintain “Buy”). I look forward to HARMONi-3 and HARMONi-7 trial data, details on a licensing deal, and the company’s strategy for ivonescimab outside of NSCLC.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Seadrill Limited: Weak Outlook, But A Cheap Way To Acquire Additional Rigs
data.description:
The Thesis

Seadrill Limited (NYSE:SDRL), one of the four major drilling contractors, is currently trading at a PE ratio of around 4.90, which is the lowest compared to its peers. Moreover, the company is highly regarded by Wall Street, which recommends it as a strong buy. In contrast, SA analysts rate it a sell, and the quant perspective is that it's even a strong sell. Examining the 13F filings of prominent "superinvestors" such as David Einhorn, Daniel Loeb, and Lee Ainslie reveals that they share the general consensus of Wall Street, as they are currently investors in Seadrill.

If I compare the value of Seadrill’s revenue-generating assets, its drilling units, to its market cap, I conclude that the company is trading well below the replacement value. Moreover, from an intrinsic value perspective, the company also appears to be undervalued substantially. Adding to this positive outlook are rumors that Seadrill could be a potential acquisition target.

Nevertheless, the company is facing re-contracting problems and some industry weaknesses. After analyzing some of Seadrill's competitors, namely Noble Corporation (NE) and Valaris Limited (VAL), I concluded that they present a better upside potential. Thus, I currently rate Seadrill as a hold.

Intro

Seadrill provides exploration and production services for the oil and gas industry. The company owns 17 rigs, with a clear focus on the high-demand 7th-generation drillships. The company’s marketed fleet is 77% contracted in 2025 and has a current backlog of $3 billion. Seadrill’s CEO, Simon Johnson, and his management team follow a clear business strategy: maintaining a strong balance sheet and returning cash to their shareholders.

Let me start by looking at the health of the balance sheet. The company currently has $618 million in debt, $2.90 billion in equity, and $404 million in cash and short-term investments. With a debt-to-equity ratio of 21% and an interest coverage ratio of 2.26, the company has sufficient means to cover its interest payments. Looking at shareholder returns, the company has repurchased a substantial amount of its stock since 2023. Repurchasing a total of $795 million, effectively reducing the share count by over 22%.

Market Size

According to Precedence Research, the offshore drilling market is currently valued at around $43 billion. Growing at a CAGR of around 8%, the market size is expected to increase to approximately $86 billion in 2034. This growth is primarily driven by increased energy needs resulting from economic and population growth, especially in China, the Middle East, and India.

Oil and gas remain essential to meet those increased energy needs. While renewables will continue to play a more significant role going forward, oil and gas are estimated to account for around 50% of the global energy supply through 2050. Due to its relatively low breakeven price, Deepwater production is expected to play an increasingly important role. While total upstream investments are expected to remain flat, deepwater investment is set to grow. One of the major offshore drillers, Valaris, estimates that deepwater production will increase approximately 23% from 2024 to 2030.

Current Market Weakness

The two most important metrics in the offshore drilling industry are rig utilization and day rates. Rig utilization is an indicator of the number of contracted rigs in operation. The higher the rig utilization, the stronger the demand for offshore projects and vice versa. Day rates reflect the amount of money rig operators are getting paid daily. Day rates are determined by the dynamics of supply and demand. A high demand increases day rates, while a low demand puts downward pressure on day rates.

Noble's CEO, Robert Eifler, has expressed that he and his team are currently seeing a mid-cycle “lull”, which started around the second half of 2023, and is expected to continue well into 2025 and possibly 2026. Valaris CEO, Anton Dibowitz, and his team explain that they are witnessing a decline in the pace of contracting and an elevated amount of deferred projects. As reasons, Anton Dibowitz names increased capital discipline of their customers, limited supply of production equipment, and regulatory approvals. He further explains that it is important to note that the projects are being delayed, not canceled. When asked about his view on the outlook of future projects, he explains that he and his team feel good about the number of opportunities and the ongoing talks they are having with their customers. Similar comments have been made by Seadrill’s CEO, Simon Johnson. For now, customers focus on returning capital to their shareholders and/or reducing their debt, rather than allocating capital to offshore projects.

The comments from these CEOs are in line with the data from the graph above. There is a clear upward trend starting in February 2021 and lasting until the end of 2024, reaching a high of approximately 93%. Afterwards, a downward trend begins, with utilization declining to approximately 85%. Starting at the beginning of 2025, utilization increased and currently stands at around 88%, which might suggest a reversal, back to an upward trend.

By examining day rates, the picture becomes less clear. The last offshore cycle peaked in 2014 and 2015, with day rates reaching well above $600.000. The collapse of oil prices between 2012 and 2016 led to a decline in the demand for offshore projects, dragging rig utilization and day rates down with them (average oil prices decreased from around $111.63 in 2012 to $43.67 in 2016). Starting in 2021, after most economies reopened following the COVID-19 pandemic, oil prices skyrocketed, reaching a price above $100 in 2022. Higher oil prices led to an increase in the demand for offshore projects, pushing rig utilization and day rates up. In the graph above, there is a clear upward trend between 2021 and the start of 2024. The upward trend leveled off in mid-2024 and is currently going down.

Examining both metrics, there is a definite pause in the current cycle, but there are no clear indications that the cycle has reversed into a downward trend. I would conclude that the comments from the CEOs are somewhat accurate, and we are currently experiencing some weakness and a mid-cycle “lull”. Further data is needed to better assess the future direction of the trend.

Seadrill, a potential acquisition target?

Consolidation in the offshore drilling industry

Since several offshore drillers emerged from bankruptcy, the industry has come under pressure. It has become increasingly difficult for offshore drillers to grow through internal investments, as prices for new rigs have skyrocketed.

The current price for building new 7th-generation drillships exceeds $850 million and has a delivery time of more than three years. Moreover, some research suggests that by now the costs are north of $1 billion. According to Noble CEO Robert Eifler, such a project would require a contract over 10 years with day rates of approximately $650.000. Matt Lyne (Valaris Chief Commercial Officer) believes that there will not be another new-build cycle of floaters in the foreseeable future due to high build costs, long lead times, and limited shipyard availability.

The difficulties companies face in purchasing new rigs have led to an increase in industry consolidation. Over the past couple of years, several companies in the industry have been acquired. For example, Noble has been on a buying spree, acquiring Pacific Drilling in 2021, Maersk Drilling in 2022, and Diamond Offshore Drilling in 2024. Seadrill is no stranger to acquisitions, either. They acquired Aquadrill in 2022.

The consolidation increased the acquirer's market share, thus increasing market dominance and efficiency. In my analysis of Valaris Limited, I explained how the company leverages its fleet size to capitalize on the multi-year upward cycle. In my other analysis, I explained how Noble Corporation is leveraging its position by focusing on the high-demand 7th-generation drill ships. Bottom line, a large and state-of-the-art fleet enables a company to bid on more contracts, making it more attractive to its customers and thus increasing revenues.

The Value of Seadrill’s drilling units

Seadrill operates a fleet of 17 rigs. The company directly owns 15 of them and manages two rigs (the Libongos and the Quenguela) owned by Sonadrill Holding Ltd. To get a sense of the value of Seadrill’s rigs, I took a look at the balance sheet of their latest filing and found that they value their drilling units at $2.96 billion. In addition to that number, I attempted to determine current market prices for their rigs. The following table contains information about Seadrill’s fleet and estimates of the prices at which the different rigs could be sold. Those estimates are based on research from Esgian and the latest sales of rigs by various operators. Moreover, I assumed that, since most of the rigs are still pretty new and state-of-the-art, they will not be scrapped (scrapped rigs sell for a small fraction of the prices in the table below).

Mainly, prices are impacted by the following factors:

  • The generation: The latest generations are more valuable
  • The age: Younger rigs are more valuable than older rigs
  • The state: Active rigs are more valuable than stacked rigs
  • The amount of time the rig has been stacked: Rigs that have been stacked for multiple years are less valuable.
  • The condition: Rigs in better condition can be sold for higher prices
  • Operational history: Rigs with an efficient operational history sell for higher prices.
  • The Type: Drillships are worth more than semisubmersibles, which in turn are more valuable than jackups.
  • The use: Rigs sold to competitors are worth more than when they are sold for scrapping.

Status: Active

Generation and Year

Value (in $ millions)

Sevan Louisiana (Semisubmersible)

6th, built in 2013

38 - 53

West Elara (Jackup)

6th, built in 2011

35 - 60

West Auriga (Drillship)

7th, built in 2013

160 - 175

West Carina (Drillship)

7th, built in 2015

170 - 185

West Jupiter (Drillship)

7th, built in 2014

165 - 180

West Neptune (Drillship)

7th, built in 2014

165 - 180

West Saturn (Drillship)

7th, built in 2014

165 - 180

West Tellus (Drillship)

7th, built in 2013

160 - 175

West Vela (Drillship)

7th, built in 2013

160 - 175

West Gemini (Drillship)

6th, built in 2010

140 -160

West Polaris (Drillship)

6th, built in 2008

60 - 75

Status: Stacked

Generation and Year

Value (in $ millions)

West Capella (Drillship)

6th, built in 2008

50 - 65

West Aquarius (Semisubmersible)

6th, built in 2008

30 - 45

West Phoenix (Semisubmersible)

6th, built in 2008

30 - 45

West Eclipse (Semisubmersible)

6th, built in 2011

33 - 46

Adding the estimates together, I arrive at a total value for Seadrill’s rigs between $1.56 billion and $1.79 billion. Even considering the high estimates, this value is considerably lower than the $2.96 billion that appears on Seadrill’s balance sheet. I am not surprised by this, as I expected the number to differ due to accounting rules and high fluctuations in market prices for rigs.

Current mood towards consolidation

Seadrill’s CEO, Simon Johnson, explained that due to their clean balance sheet and the healthy backlog, they are a potential acquisition target. Moreover, several competing CEOs are in favor of further industry consolidation.

First, rumors have already circulated about a potential merger between Seadrill and Transocean. At the time when the rumors were reported, both companies saw their share prices increase by 10% and 4% respectively. The merger of those two leading offshore drillers would create the biggest offshore fleet in the industry, surpassing the fleets of Valaris and Noble. But for now, nothing materialized out of those rumors.

Second, Valaris CEO Anton Dibowitz mentioned in an earnings call that he and his team are very pro-M&A as it is a way to add high-spec capacity to the company’s fleet and create value for shareholders. So we might have another company that's willing to look for an acquisition.

If we consider all the major players, we must also mention Noble. Although Noble has been on a buying spree over the past few years, I don't think they will pursue any acquisitions over the next couple of months. Noble just finished integrating Diamond's fleet into Noble's enterprise resource planning system, basically completing the last integration processes between the two former companies.

Why an Acquisition would make sense

As mentioned, the probability of major offshore contractors ordering new ships is very low. So, an attractive way of adding additional rigs to a company’s fleet is by acquiring another company. What makes Seadrill’s fleet a potentially interesting acquisition target is the value of its combined drilling units compared to the cost of new builds. If we assume that the price of a new-built floater is approximately $850.000 with delivery times of more than 3 years, it could be far cheaper to buy a company like Seadrill, where you get 15 rigs that are operational and come with an operational history.

If Seadrill gets acquired, the buyer would not only get an attractive fleet but also a clean balance sheet and a healthy backlog of $3 billion. The company currently has $618 million in debt ($50 million of unsecured debt is due in August 2028; the rest are bonds due in August 2030), $2.90 billion in equity, and $404 million in cash and short-term investments. Those numbers result in a debt-to-equity ratio of 21% (the lowest in the industry) and an interest coverage ratio of 2.26. Looking at those numbers, I conclude that the company does not appear to be facing bankruptcy anytime soon, as it has sufficient means to cover its interest payments.

In 2025, six of Seadrill's rigs are expected to complete their current contracts. In mid-2026, two additional rigs will follow. In Seadrill’s latest earnings call, management’s outlook on future contracts was somewhat vague. They talk about ongoing talks, but nothing substantial has been announced yet. An acquisition could solve this problem. A company with a larger fleet has more leverage in contract discussions and can thus capitalize on the current uptick in rig utilization, resulting in higher day rates.

What would be Seadrill’s Acquisition price?

Acquisition prices are typically higher than the target company's current market cap. Due to the high capital intensity of the industry, premiums tend to be around 10% to 30% (those percentages are based on past deals). Examining past deals, I concluded that the premium tends to be higher when the target company’s rigs are young and modern, which is the case for Seadrill. Applying such a premium of 20% to 30% to Seadrill’s current market cap of $1.63 billion would place the deal somewhere between $1.96 billion and $2.12 billion.

To gain a better understanding of Seadrill’s value, I often rely on a DCF analysis. In Seadrill’s case, there is a lot of uncertainty. While I am convinced that the offshore drilling industry is currently in an upward cycle, the outlook for Seadrill does not look as clear. I am a bit worried about the re-contracting issues of all the rigs that roll off their contracts this year and its four stacked rigs. Maintaining them, in the case when they need to be stacked due to an issue with not finding a contract, costs a lot of money.

To value Seadrill, I use the following assumptions:

  • I expect revenues to decline over the next year. Management shared its full-year revenue guidance, expecting revenues to come in between $1.30 billion and $1.36 billion. I am on the lower end of those estimates and expect a revenue decline of around 5% for 2025 compared to 2024. In the following years, revenue growth is expected to pick up again, based on the assumption that the industry is in an upcycle. I expect revenues to grow at a mid-single-digit growth rate.
  • The growth rate is fixed at 2% in perpetuity.
  • I assume an initial reinvestment rate of around 170%, decreasing gradually and reaching 24% in perpetuity. Higher reinvestments will be required over the next 3 to 4 years due to the reactivation of stacked rigs. Afterwards, reinvestment needs will decrease as they consist of maintaining and updating rigs.
  • Operating margins are expected to improve gradually, peaking at an average of 30%.
  • I assume that operating margins will remain around 25% in perpetuity.
  • I expect the company to generate ROIC equal to its cost of capital.
  • I will set the cost of capital to 9.91% and gradually decrease it to 8.38% which is equal to the industry average.

Using a discounted cash flow model under the above assumptions, I arrive at a value of $39.23 per share. This would give the company an estimated market cap of $2.44 billion. If we use this estimated value and add a premium of around 20% to 30%, the acquisition price could fluctuate somewhere between $2.92 billion and $3.17 billion. The upper end would put the value of the deal slightly below Noble’s acquisition of Maersk Drilling.

Conclusion

From a pure investment perspective, Seadrill appears to be a decent opportunity. If the assumptions I used to value the company play out, an investment in Seadrill would result in a potential 40% upside. Moreover, when you buy the stock, you also get the option of Seadrill being acquired by one of the larger players in the offshore drilling industry. This could push the price above my estimated intrinsic value.

However, if you are looking to capitalize on the offshore drilling upcycle, there are better investments to be made (consider Noble or Valaris). Finally, a potential bet on the idea that Seadrill might be acquired is also somewhat risky, in my opinion. So, for me, I rate Seadrill as a hold.

data.title: SPSM: Small Caps Are Unlikely To Outperform
data.description:
The SPDR Portfolio S&P 600 Small Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:SPSM), launched on 07/08/2013 by State Street Global Advisors, Inc. and managed by SSGA Funds Management, Inc., provides exposure to the small-cap segment of the U.S. equity market by tracking the performance of the S&P SmallCap 600 Index. It charges a 0.03% expense ratio and manages about $12 billion.

Small-cap stocks offer an aggressive approach to investing due to their higher sensitivity to interest rates and credit conditions. And this approach is not warranted right now. Besides the fact that the macro environment suggests challenges for small caps in the near term, large caps are likely to outperform them because of the strength in the tech sector to which they are biased and their more muted response to macro challenges.

Portfolio

The portfolio offered by SPSM certainly has some advantages that I do appreciate in an ETF, the primary being the fewer unpredictable swings its price can have due to the lack of meaningful non-systemic factors. The top 10 holdings are just 5.69% of the fund, and there's none that accounts for more than 0.72%.

But as I explained in the introduction, small caps are more sensitive to interest rate changes than large caps. Besides the fact that they underlie small businesses that don't have the same access to credit that larger ones do, financial institutions represent a larger chunk of this market segment.

Performance

It makes sense that small caps struggle to outperform large caps in the long run. Since SPSM's inception, it has lagged behind them and the return differential only keeps widening.

More recently, its YTD performance has been worse on both an absolute and risk-adjusted basis. SPSM is still in the red, as it didn't recover as much as large caps since the April drawdown.

Outlook

The thing is that this is unlikely to change. Especially now that the market enjoys a break from rapid shifts in trade policy and growth implications.

Speaking of growth, let us begin with understanding the growth narrative to better predict the relative strength or vulnerability of small-cap stocks.

First of all, if you look at the GDP QoQ change, you will notice that there was a 0.5% linked-quarter contraction in the first quarter of 2025. That is significantly different than the 1.5% growth noted by the Final Sales to Domestic Purchasers. That's because the latter excludes net exports, pointing to pre-tariff inventory build-up as the primary cause of the divergence.

The front-loading activity businesses engaged in before tariffs were imposed also removes any positive signal from the uptrend the ISM Manufacturing New Orders Index experienced in April and May. As you can see, the YTD trend points to declining orders for manufactured goods, and the latest data point confirms that aggregate demand is weakening.

Economists forecast slower growth in the second and third quarters and an improving pace afterwards, indicating that the growth challenges will be more modest in the long run, but meaningful in the short term.

On Thursday, we got the latest jobs report from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, which puts the nonfarm payroll YoY growth slightly higher than it was last month. Economists expected 110,000 jobs to be added in June, but the report shows that the U.S. added 147,000 instead. While that was pleasantly surprising, the unemployment rate falling by 10 bps to 4.1% coincides with a 130K drop in the labor force and doesn't necessarily reflect hiring strength; the labor force participation rate has contracted by 0.3% YoY.

Moreover, while the job openings rate is now close to the level at the start of the year, the unemployment insurance claims have meaningfully risen YTD, suggesting a potential higher unemployment in the near term. Besides, businesses are reluctant to hire and hesitant to reduce headcount, as the labor market may be softening but is still tight. As long as economic challenges persist (and they are likely to) and the market keeps cooling, this could change, adding upward pressure to the unemployment rate.

The labor market challenges are followed by inflation ones. While the latest PCE readings show levels close to the Fed's target, we have to merely look at how likely it is that this will persist.

Fed economists have found evidence of a two-month lag between tariff implementation and higher prices. This is supported by the reasonable hypothesis that businesses have to exhaust their pre-tariff inventory before they start passing on the higher costs to consumers in order to preserve margins. So, it's still early to declare victory. Inflation expectations are reflective of this thesis.

The central bank is justified in not cutting further the Fed Funds rate then. The labor market is cooling but still strong enough; meanwhile, inflation data have yet to capture the effects of tariffs and the Fed is most likely to wait longer before reducing the policy interest rate. Be that as it may, FOMC participants forecast two more cuts this year, which indicates that the labor market is going to need support soon and that higher inflation is not going to be a long-term issue.

Fixed-income markets reflect a scenario in which a recession doesn't occur, interest rates go down, and credit markets don't experience any meaningful deterioration. The term spread is higher YTD, and the credit spread has been narrowing again in recent months.

Equity markets are also optimistic. The NAAIM index shows that fund managers remain bullish and the AAII index indicates an increased retail investor exposure level in June, now closer to the one at the start of the year.

So, what we have here is short-term growth and inflation challenges that have yet to surface in the hard data that move markets, paired with now very optimistic market participants. This places all stocks in a fragile position, but small-cap ones lack the strength provided by the AI names that dominate the broader market. Later this year, lower interest rates may change the relative potential, but for now, large-cap funds, although introducing higher idiosyncratic risks, will most likely keep outperforming small-cap ones like SPSM because of the AI narrative and their lower sensitivity to interest-rate change implications.

Your Turn

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

Thank you for reading.
